speaker: interviewer
text: Ruth, you were telling us about your life in the bunker while you were in the woods. Can you describe a typical day and tell us how you got food, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: We was going stealing, to-- to friends. We used to buy-- when we can used to have a little bit-- we used to steal a cow, kill it, and sell meat to other people. You know, everybody have a few dollars-- a few marks. And this way we can buy bread. We used to buy bread, just bread, no milk. And we used to cook potatoes We used to go steal potatoes-- not buy. We used to cook water make potatoes to eat for dinner. Sometimes we make the-- like, if we got a little bit flour, we made, like, dumplings, with water and potatoes. And meat. We used to have meat. Sometimes we have more meat, we-- we can take it and we have it there. Till '44 June, no, May, start to be bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now, and-- and before, while you were hiding in the bunker, were the Germans nearby? How far-- you know, how worried--

speaker: interviewee
text: They used to come. They used to come. They used to be in the city. You know what I mean? And they used to come. The flights used to go by and throw bombs in the woods. They find out the Partizaners are in the woods. And that's what I was doing. We used to have Russian people there, Polish people, Ukrainian people, and Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: All in the woods, in the same woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: In woods, mixed

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and how much contact did you have with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: We don't have too much contact with the-- with the Polish people. And the Ukrainian was bad. They was-- liked to kill everybody. Well, we was taking care-- you know, most-- they know they can't start with us. We used to have guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who knew where you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: The people-- people knowed. Well, was everybody told each other. I mean, the German knowed. The people from the country-- what I lived of the country-- like [INAUDIBLE] they know that Jews are hiding in the woods. Or we used to come buying bread from them. One lady took four guys. She says she's selling potatoes to them and brought the Gestapo, and they shot 'em there. Sure, they know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now approximately how long were you in the woods, altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: A year.

speaker: interviewer
text: A year. OK. And did all eight people who were hiding with you survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- we-- our group, eight, about 20 survived. I mean, our group. Another group survived. Lots of groups, not everybody survived. Some died from typhus. Some was killed in the woods.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me exactly who was hiding with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my boyfriend, his mother, his brother, my mother's cousin, with her husband. Friends was Schreiber-- Ruth Schreiber was, with two kids. Sarah Schnapp, [INAUDIBLE] Schnapp, was with a little girl, seven-year-old girl. Jacob Koener, with his wife, with a little boy, six years old, in the hiding woods.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was a group that we was together. The little boy, six years old, is now a man having children. And the first time when I saw him in New York, at a bar mitzvah, he comes up to me. He's a man. You know, how should I know? How can I recognize? I no saw him for years. He said to me, Ruthie, you-- you don't know who I am? I said, I should know who you are? He said, you're supposed to know. You gave me eat once in a while. You carried me. You know, his mother can't carry him. He was a six-year-old little boy-- we run from one place to another. We carried--

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I am Jacob Koener's son. Oy. When he told me that, I was so shocked. I mean, we was last year of our 50th anniversary, he came up, kissed me, hugging me. His mother's about 100 years old. She's still living. The father died, about 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful. OK, so can you tell me what happened, that you came out of hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the Russian soldiers us liberated. And they took us off a truck-- off the back, not keep us from the front-- took us back a little bit. In a city that we was afraid the Germans should not come back-- not get-- you're not-- you're in again with the Germans. That was, like, a week.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1944, July, end of July. In August-- we came home, beginning August. The Russians was very nice to us. They went-- how much boys was left, took them all to the service. But my husband was shot by the Germans, when he ran away from the concentration camp, in his left arm. I have the picture. My daughter-in-law saw it yesterday. She said, that's the scar. Yes, he went in hospital. He was in hospital. And my boyfriend, my cousins, friends-- about 30 people-- guys-- the Russian took to the service and sent them straight to the front. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I was left with my mother-in-law. She was not, that time, my mother-in-law. Well, I stayed with her. My boyfriend told me I should watch his mother. They went to the service. He went, and they sign papers who he leaves. He went. He told them he left a wife and a mother. He no told he left a wife, a mother, and a girlfriend. 1940, for that, I came already in-- in the city, after they went to the service [INAUDIBLE], I came to the city where I was afraid to stay and where I was born. For the Ukraine was Partizaner still. And when I came to the city, I have nothing to eat. I was starving. After the war, after everything nice and set, we was liberated-- Ruthie, you're going through still hell. You're not still set. You lost everybody. You have no money. You're walking with wooden shoes. You're wearing-- you took from a-- a-- a soldier, a uniform. Lady, she give it to me, a blouse and  a skirt, and I'm wearing that. And I'm starving from hunger. And I went across the street, to a pump, to pump water. And I say, I boil the water. And I go up to some-- a friend. Maybe she have something to make soup for me, for my mother-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm pumping the water. Comes out a guy. His name was Haskel. And we was together in hiding woods. Well, he was not with our group, but I knowed him. And he comes out. He say, whoa, what are you doing here? I say, I'm pumping water, and I have nothing to eat. I said, goddamn-- what are you doing here? He said, I'm a chef for the Russian and covered the cooking, making dinner-- lunch, dinner, breakfast. He say, you know how to milk cows? I say, sure, I know how. I was a farm girl. I milked cows. He said, OK, spill out the water and go milk the cows. I said, oh, no. I'm not gonna milk a cow till I eat something. I'm starving, and I need to take something to my mother-in-law. She was not my mother-in-law. I say, I need to take to [? Sheindl ?] something. Her name was [? Sheindl. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, OK, go take her food. He gave me a plate. I ate first and took a plate to my mother-in-law. And I came back with a bucket, and I milked the cows. There was, like, five cows. I brought in a full bucket milk.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, now I'm taking home a half a bucket  of milk. He say, why? I think, I sell it, and I have money to buy food. He say, you're crazy. If they catch me and catch you, they'll kill us both-- the Russian soldier. I said, don't worry. And I went, and I took a half a bucket of milk, and I sold it right away, just, like, 10 rubles a quart, just to make money. And came. I was every time loving from money. I like every time money. And came to the-- that was in the evening. In the morning, he said to me, you're coming in the morning? I say, yeah. He said, they pay 80 rubles a month. You want to work here? I said, I have nothing what to do. Why not?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I milked three times a day the cows. And I worked in the kitchen. I took home milk. And the Russian went-- brought chicken for them. They went to the country. And they took away from the people. Chickens, they brought. They brought cows, to kill-- the meat-- to eat. They need food. They no care from whom they took, the Russian. And I-- I killed the chickens. I flipped them. I put in  the hot water-- flipped them and took all my chicken-- sold it. I mean, I start to making money and that helped.

speaker: interviewee
text: After a while, was a brewer in that place. They used to make spirit. And I got the guy was selling spirit. I say, how much you want for a quart spirit? He said, 80 rubles.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, good. Give me-- give me two bottles spirit. Well, I need empty bottles. From the spirit, I make four quarts whiskey. And I sold for 80 rubles that. And that's how I used to make money. And I worked in the restaurant, saving the money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was, like, six months. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] February of 14, my boyfriend was killed in the war. The day before Pesach-- Erev Pesach-- came to this two soldiers with a letter. As I told you, he signed me-- I was his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, Ruthie, you have a letter. Your husband was a hero. I say, what do you mean, a hero? He went in Berlin-- that-and-that hill, he was killed. I said, that's why you came, to tell me he was a hero? And I fainted. My mother-in-law start to scream. How come he mark me a wife and not his mother-- and he no mark the mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was wrong, too. When he would be marked, that she's he left a mother, she would got pension. By that, she no got nothing. And when I would have a child, I would got pension. But I no got nothing, either. I have no children. As we was, my husband came home from the hospital. And we thought for sure he lost his arm. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The doctor was a good doctor-- took out four inches bone from his arm-- from my husband's arm. He have the arm, but he cannot work too much. And then he came home, like, in May. June 11th, we got married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us his name, again?

speaker: interviewee
text: David.

speaker: interviewer
text: David Weitz. And what was his date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 16, 1920.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and where was he born?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Pohorylce.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now, he came home from the hospital? And you got married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we was still by the Russian, a whole summer. I was working the restaurant, and I was selling whiskey. And we just-- and everybody was leaving the city to go to Poland, to Krakow. Lots of people, Jewish people, left the Russian and went to Poland. We decided to go, too. And a lots of Polish people was leaving Ukraine, to come Poland. We went off a train. We were the only Jews of that train. It was, like, 400 people. Was about, like, 200 families.

speaker: interviewee
text: We came to Krakow, and we went to see-- we have there family-- cousins I know. You know what I mean? My husband left me on the train-- well, the train stopped there, like, four hours. And he went there to see to we can take our stuff and go be stay in Krakow. With my one cousin, stay in Krakow. We cannot find an apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Go to Byten, the Germans' side, already. Byten was the Germany half. We came there. There is-- my cousin was one, with her husband and her brother-in-law. His name was Chaim, and her name is [? Henia, ?] Her husband, my cousin's husband, died in Israel.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went there. They gave us the address. We were there. We went there, and he find us an apartment in Byten We walked in in that apartment. Was already November, I think. November? November. Was beginning November or end of-- you know, October-- something like that. And, when I saw the apartment, I start to cry. He said, why are you crying? I said, it's a mess here. Look at the apartment. Was some furniture-- put together-- broken, cold, no coals to heat the room. No nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I have money. I no care. I said, go first buy, off the black market, food. We have something to eat. We're warm and coal. Bring that. And he brought that thing. Took us a whole night to put together everything. It was nice.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother-in-law not move to nothing. She got to be, like-- I don't know what happened. Just complete-- we're supposed to take care of her. And we walked out two days later, he say, you know what happened? I signed up in a kibbutz. I said, what do you mean you signed up? And Leipzig is at the kibbutz. And we be four weeks there, we can go to Israel. I said, that's fine with me. But I can't sign you you're my wife. I need to sign you you're my sister. They no took in couples. They was afraid you get pregnant or something. I said, fine with me, too. OK? But first we need to go of the American zone. We need to go through black market. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK? We need to go to Prague. We need to go other places, nighttime. And we cannot carry too much-- just what you can take off your back. I said, fine with me, again. Said, what do you do with your mother? I don't care. I'm young. I can take everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: He say, she be against in the kibbutz with 20 kids, children, little children. And she be them like a mother to them. They take care of her. We don't need to pay for her. But I need-- you need to pay for your own trip.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, how much need to cost me? He said, 5,000 marks. I said, I can get in the kibbutz. I don't need to pay nothing. He said, well, you need to stay with my mother. I don't have nobody to stay with my mother. I said, I don't care. And we started to fight. But the nicer thing is, I have no choice, and I paid my 5,000 marks. And we're coming in the train, the guy say, pay for your mother 5,000. You paid for your sister, you need to pay for your mother. I said to him, Dave, what happen now? He say, I can't tell you the whole story.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well we came to Munich with the train. First we went through in Prague. We was there for four days. And after Prague, we went off a train and came and going through the-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the custom. And I have a few things from home and they took away.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a candelabra. I have the cup from the wine. I have a material I got, what I bought to make myself a couple dresses. Well, I don't know how to sew, but I figure I'd come someplace-- somebody would do it for me. And I came there, and they took away that. You can't buy it. We came in the train, and we went to Munich, of the American zone. When we passed that-- that station, and they say, you are in the American zone, not in Germany, I picked up, and I screamed. Was a full wagon with people. I said, thank God we left everybody. I'm free. And we came to Munich. I am broke. I have no money. Spent all money. Worked for a whole year, and spent stupid. Stupid, that was stupid, some things, but you can't help it. Come in, and the main office was-- and when I saw what happened to that state-- I never saw it-- was a disaster. Come up, and I see a friend-- Jacob, the guy what we was in hiding with, with his son.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, Jacob. I'm glad you're here. He say, why? He hugged me-- kissed me. He say, you came late. What happened? I said, don't ask what happened. I'll tell you another time. While we're waiting to be shipped in a DP camp, I say, I need money. He say, how much you need? I say give me two, three hundred dollars. And when I can settle, I'll be working-- I do something-- I'll bring you-- pay you back. I'll find out what camp you are. He was in a different camp. He say, fine. He took out three-hundred dollars and give it. I have money. And my husband hollers of me. He say, you cannot live a day out money. You need to have that money. I say, Dave, please. Just leave me alone. I was upset-- not with the money. I don't know, you know, where are we going? With a sick husband, with an arm-- not an arm. That was, for me, crazy to get married. But you were in love, so you got married. With a mother-in-law. Well, she no pitch nothing. And I'm going. And I was upset with everything. I came-- it was December-- in that DP camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We're going in kitchen with a container for food. What do you think God helps you? Everything nice and dandy?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came there, December. January, I got pregnant. I was pregnant not normal. I got sick. The second day, pregnancy, I walked in the kitchen. I can't stand the smell. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Another problem-- I lived off nine months candy, sour candy, sour apples. That was not enough. I see-- that's no life. And I said to my husband, that's not life for me. I need to try to do something.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, were you also in a DP camp, at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one, do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Leipheim, Germany, not too far from Munich. And I come there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and the nicer thing happen. They say to me that-- people was already there, you know, in camp, like, a month, two months, just opened the new camp. Was American soldiers. Now they don't know Jewish. We don't know English. They don't know Polish. They don't know Ukrainian. They don't know German. They don't know nothing. Go talk to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: One Jewish guy-- a soldier-- was Jewish, and he knowed a little bit. He comes up to me. He say, you married? I say, yeah. He say, how come a pretty girl got married, so quick after the war? I say, well, I was lonesome, and that's what happened. Don't talk too much. He say to me-- he opened a-- a-- a [INAUDIBLE] which learn how to sew. You know how to sew? I say, yeah. I know how crocheting, I know how to knit. Needlepoint, I know that. But from sewing, forget it. He say to me, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go learn a trade. When you go to the United States, you need that. And did you follow his advice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Camed. I walked in-- in there. There was one big room-- was a nice guy-- an older one, in the 50s. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We was, like, 20 people working. And he say to me-- to us, I teach you how to sew. We need sew up men's clothes, uniforms. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And that's what happened. That's how I learned how to sew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
